title: Multi-modal RAG with Captioning (Vision-LLM Image Summaries Indexed as Text)
one_liner: Extracts images from PDFs, captions them with a vision LLM (Gemini 1.5 Flash) into retrieval-optimized text summaries, and indexes those captions alongside page text in one Chroma vector store so figures and tables become searchable with ordinary text embeddings.
problem_solved: Text-only RAG pipelines silently drop figures, charts, diagrams, and tables that are embedded as images in PDFs, so any question whose answer lives in a visual element retrieves nothing useful. This technique converts visual content into text at index time (image -> caption/summary) so it becomes retrievable and answerable through a standard text-embedding pipeline, without adopting a multimodal embedding model.
failure_modes: My documents contain figures, charts, or diagrams and my RAG system can't answer questions about them
Answers that live only in a table or figure of a PDF are never retrieved
PDF text extraction ignores images, so visual content is invisible to the retriever
Questions about specific values shown in charts or result tables (e.g. 'what is the BLEU score of the base model?') come back unanswered or hallucinated
I need one unified index over both text and visual content but my embedding model is text-only
when_to_use: Your corpus is PDFs (papers, financial reports, manuals, slide exports) where key facts appear in figures or tables rendered as embedded images
You want to keep a standard text-embedding vector store and query-time latency of plain RAG, paying the multimodal cost once at index time
You have access to a vision-capable LLM for offline captioning (Gemini, GPT-4o, Claude) and an embedding service
Users ask factual questions whose answers appear in experiment tables, benchmark charts, or labeled diagrams
Index build is infrequent and image counts are moderate, so per-image captioning cost is acceptable
tradeoffs: One vision-LLM call per extracted image at index time: build cost and time scale linearly with image count
Captioning is lossy compression: any visual detail the summary does not verbalize is unrecoverable at query time; dense tables or fine-grained charts may lose exact values
The notebook stores only the caption text, not a pointer back to the raw image, so generation answers from the caption rather than re-inspecting the original figure
Depends on three external services (Google Gemini for captioning, Cohere for embeddings and generation, Chroma locally): multiple API keys and vendor coupling
PyMuPDF page.get_images() only finds embedded raster images; vector-drawn charts and tables laid out as text get no special handling

Retrieval in the notebook is naive similarity with k=1 and no reranking or grounding checks, which is brittle; the generation prompt even says 'answer based upon your knowledge', weakening faithfulness
Do not use if your documents are text-only (plain RAG is cheaper) or if visual fidelity is critical, where ColPali-style page-image embeddings that retrieve the actual page image are a better fit
how_it_works: Index time (dominant stage): PyMuPDF (fitz) opens the PDF and, per page, captures the raw text (stored as a Document with page-number metadata) and extracts every embedded raster image via page.get_images(full=True) + pdf_file.extract_image(xref), saving each with PIL. Each saved image is sent to Gemini 1.5 Flash with a prompt instructing it to produce a concise summary of the table/image/text 'well optimized for retrieval'; the returned caption becomes its own text Document. Both page-text Documents and caption Documents are split with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_tiktoken_encoder (chunk_size=400, overlap=50), embedded with Cohere embed-english-v3.0, and loaded into a single Chroma collection, so image-derived text competes in the same semantic space as body text. Query time: a plain similarity retriever (k=1 in the notebook) fetches the best chunk, which may be an image caption, and a LangChain chain (ChatPromptTemplate | ChatCohere command-r-plus | StrOutputParser) generates a short answer from it. Retrieval and generation stages are otherwise vanilla; all the technique's leverage is in the captioning-based indexing.
key_dependencies: pymupdf (fitz) for PDF text and image extraction
langchain / langchain-core / langchain-community for orchestration, prompts, Document, text splitter
key_code_signals: fitz.open(pdf) with page.get_text() and page.get_images(full=True) / pdf_file.extract_image(xref) — dual text+image extraction loop
model.generate_content([image, 'You are an assistant tasked with summarizing tables, images and text for retrieval...']) — retrieval-optimized caption prompt per image
CohereEmbeddings(model="embed-english-v3.0") — text embeddings applied to both page text and captions
Chroma.from_documents(documents=doc_splits + img_splits, collection_name="multi_model_rag", ...) — single mixed text+caption index
prompt | ChatCohere(model="command-r-plus", temperature=0) | StrOutputParser() — plain LCEL generation chain
related_techniques: multi_model_rag_with_colpali: alternative — embeds PDF page images directly with a vision retrieval model instead of converting images to lossy text captions
simple_rag: refines — extends the baseline text-only RAG pipeline to documents containing visual content
document_augmentation: analogous — both generate synthetic text at index time to make hard-to-retrieve content retrievable
reranking: composes — the naive k=1 similarity retrieval over a mixed text+caption index would benefit from reranking
context_enrichment_window_around_chunk: composes — small 400-token chunks could be expanded with neighboring context after retrieval
contextual_chunk_headers: composes — captions and page chunks could carry document/section headers to disambiguate mixed-source chunks
summary: Technique for making figures, charts, and tables inside PDFs retrievable in a standard text-based RAG pipeline. At index time, PyMuPDF extracts each page's text and every embedded raster image; each image is sent to Gemini 1.5 Flash with a prompt requesting a concise summary 'optimized for retrieval'. These captions are treated as ordinary text documents: both page text and captions are split into 400-token chunks (50 overlap), embedded with Cohere embed-english-v3.0, and stored together in one Chroma collection. Query time is unchanged plain RAG: similarity retrieval (k=1 in the notebook) followed by answer generation with Cohere command-r-plus. Solves the failure where answers living only in a figure or results table are never retrieved by text-only pipelines, without requiring multimodal embeddings. Costs: one vision-LLM call per image at build time, and lossy captions — details not verbalized are unrecoverable, and the raw image is not linked back for generation. Prefer ColPali-style page-image embedding when visual fidelity matters; skip entirely for text-only corpora.
notes_for_chunking: Well-structured, short notebook (23 cells) with clean one-line markdown headers mapping 1:1 to pipeline stages: 'Data Extraction', 'Image Captioning', 'Vectostore' (note the typo — normalize to 'Vectorstore'), 'Query', 'Output'. Cells 1-2 form a self-contained Overview/Key Components/Motivation/Method/Benefits block that is ideal card-summary material. Code cells are short (5-25 lines) and each depends on state from prior cells (text_data/img_data flow into the vectorstore cell), so stage-level chunks should include their header plus code cell(s). Junk to strip: cell 0 (promotional book advertisement), the pip-install cell, the wget download cell and its verbose transfer log output, load_dotenv boilerplate, an SVG diagram reference, and the final view-tracker image cell. Outputs are tiny except the wget log; no base64 blobs. Markdown-to-code ratio is roughly 50/50; markdown is descriptive and honest to the code.
